rescue_reasoning: Keep because the policy explicitly targets reduced building energy use by incentivizing ultra-low/net-zero energy construction. Evidence: policy_statement/verbatim_text: 'Offer monetary and/or non-monetary incentives to promote the construction of ULE or NZE buildings.' This is an energy-use reduction intervention consistent with resource/energy efficiency in buildings. The gate failure cites missing outcome token, but climate_screen is 'explicit_self', so the row is clearly climate-relevant and should remain.
policy_statement: Offer monetary and/or non-monetary incentives to promote construction of ULE or NZE buildings.
mechanism_description: Offer monetary and/or non-monetary incentives to promote construction of ultra-low energy or net-zero energy buildings, lowering building energy use and related emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Same building service (comfort/lighting/hot water) → ↓ energy input (high-performance/NZE new builds) → ↓ GHG emissions from energy supply
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in activity/system: Developers/builders are incentivized to design and construct new buildings to ultra-low-energy or net-zero standards (better envelope, passive design, efficient HVAC/lighting/appliances, controls; often paired with on-site renewables).  
(2) Mediating process: Improved building physics and equipment efficiency reduce heating/cooling and plug-load demand; reduced delivered energy means less upstream generation/combustion.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower lifecycle operational GHG emissions (and often peak-demand reduction), primarily through reduced energy consumption; any additional mitigation from on-site renewables is secondary to the demand-reduction pathway for this mechanism.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced operational energy use in new buildings, leading to lower associated GHG emissions from electricity and on-site fuel consumption.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Incentives → more ULE/NZE new construction → lower operational energy demand for the same services → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: The defining lever is lowering energy input via building efficiency/performance (resource/energy efficiency). Mitigation is the downstream result, but the mechanism’s direct, dominant intervention is energy-demand reduction.  
3) Edge cases applied: If an incentive program mainly funds on-site renewables without materially reducing demand, it would shift toward Mitigation; however, the stated mechanism is explicitly ULE/NZE buildings lowering energy use, so Resource Efficiency remains dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that incentivizes net-zero/ultra-low-energy *new buildings* shares the core outcome of reduced operational energy demand, regardless of jurisdiction or incentive type.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether specific policies emphasize electrification, renewable supply, embodied carbon, resilience, or demand response requires policy-level evidence and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Edge case: “Net-zero” can be achieved via on-site/off-site renewable procurement with only modest efficiency gains; if the incentive is structured that way, the dominant pathway could become Mitigation (clean energy substitution) rather than Resource Efficiency.
key_indicators: "Offer monetary and/or non-monetary incentives"; "promote the construction"; "ULE or NZE buildings"
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: improved indoor comfort/health, reduced energy bills, local air-quality improvements, grid peak-demand reduction.
instance_edge_case_notes: Non-monetary incentives could include expedited permitting or recognition; still best classified as an incentive-program rather than a regulatory mandate or performance-standard because adoption remains voluntary.
